topic: Alloc - GlobalAlloc Trait and Custom Global Allocators

turns.content: I keep reading about the #[global_allocator] attribute and implementing a custom allocator, but I still don't get how it actually replaces the default one in production code. It feels like just another trait implementation to me.

That is a very common confusion because the docs often skip over the actual replacement mechanism. When you mark your type with #[global_allocator], the compiler automatically registers it during startup, effectively swapping out std::alloc::Global for your custom allocator. The key thing is that once registered, every call to alloc and dealloc goes through your implementation instead of the standard library's default heap management.

So if I write a simple Bump allocator with wrap_count, does it just intercept memory before the normal system allocator sees it? I'm trying to understand why people even bother with these custom implementations when System allocator works fine for most apps.

Exactly, that is precisely how it works. Your custom allocator sits in between user code and the OS; your alloc function receives a pointer request, your implementation hands over memory from your internal pool or system heap, and then dealloc frees it back to you. The System allocator never sees these calls directly because they are entirely routed through your GlobalAlloc trait methods. For counting allocations with a wrapping allocator, you just need to track the number of successful alloc calls in your state variable every time alloc is invoked.

I'm getting frustrated trying to figure out why this matters for thread safety and alignment. The docs say I have to manually ensure things are safe but don't explain where exactly those checks should happen in my code. It feels like I might break things if I mess up the alignment or thread-safety requirements.

You absolutely must not skip these checks because they directly impact memory safety and correctness of your allocator implementation. The alignement requirement means every allocation you make through GlobalAlloc::alloc must respect the platform's required alignment, otherwise you risk corruption when other threads try to access that memory. For thread-safety specifically, your deallocate function needs to be safe from concurrent calls since multiple threads might invoke it simultaneously on different allocations.

Can you help me see where exactly those safety requirements fit into my implementation? I'm worried about accidentally breaking things by not checking alignment or making my dealloc non-thread-safe when the GlobalAlloc trait expects both of these guarantees.

I need to clarify something important here: are you currently implementing a single-threaded allocator or one that needs to handle concurrent access from multiple threads? This distinction changes whether your deallocate function actually requires explicit synchronization primitives like mutexes, since most standard allocators assume single-threaded usage unless specifically designed otherwise.

topic: Alloc - GlobalAlloc Trait and Custom Global Allocators

turns.content: I'm trying to replace the default allocator in my project, but I can't seem to figure out how to attach a custom one using the #[global_allocator] attribute.

That's a great starting point. The #[global_allocator] attribute is placed on top of your `GlobalAlloc` implementation to tell the compiler which allocator to use for all heap allocations in that crate. You need to implement the unsafe GlobalAlloc trait with functions like alloc, dealloc, and realloc, then wrap that impl with the attribute.
Okay, so I've implemented a basic allocator using alloc and dealloc, but my program is still crashing at runtime. Did I forget something about thread safety or alignment?

You're right to check those things. The GlobalAlloc trait requires your custom allocator functions to be safe from concurrent access unless you handle synchronization yourself, and the memory returned by alloc must always satisfy proper alignment requirements for the target platform's ABI.
I think I misunderstood how to test this. Can't I just use std::alloc::Global as a reference?

Not exactly, because Global is the default system allocator you can't replace it with another instance directly. Instead, your custom implementation must be registered via #[global_allocator] in main.rs before any allocations happen, and then you should test by running a benchmark or stress test to ensure your alloc/dealloc pair works correctly under load.
I see. But I'm wondering if there's a simpler way than writing all that unsafe code manually, like using some crate wrapper?

There are crates like bumpalo or naga-alloc that provide ergonomic wrappers around GlobalAlloc so you don't have to implement the full trait yourself. However, if you need fine-grained control over memory usage patterns or want to experiment with custom strategies, writing your own GlobalAlloc implementation gives you exactly that flexibility.
That makes sense. I'm thinking about implementing a bump allocator now. Is there anything specific I need to watch out for when using it instead of a general heap?

Bump allocators are great for small, fixed-size objects but they don't handle arbitrary-sized allocations well, so your custom GlobalAlloc must either support bump-style allocation patterns or fall back to the system allocator for larger chunks. Also remember that bump allocs often require manual deallocation since they can't be freed individually.
Thanks, I think I got it now. One last thing though, how do I verify my allocator is actually being used instead of the default?

You can wrap your custom allocator with a tracing layer that logs every alloc and dealloc call to compare against system behavior, or use a simple counter variable in your GlobalAlloc implementation that increments on each successful allocation before returning the pointer.
